timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jerusalem's Fall Reported

heb.paragraph: The roʿim (shepherds) is a standard ancient Near Eastern metaphor for rulers. In Mesopotamia, Sumerian and Akkadian kings bore the title 'shepherd of the people.' In Israel, God was the true shepherd (Ps 23:1) who delegated pastoral authority to human kings and leaders. Ezekiel's indictment charges Israel's shepherds with feeding themselves rather than the flock—consuming wool, milk, and meat while leaving the sheep scattered, injured, and prey to wild animals. The catalog of neglect (v. 4) systematically lists what good shepherds should do: strengthen the weak, heal the sick, bind up the injured, bring back the strayed, search for the lost. Israel's leaders did none of these.

refs.note: Jeremiah's oracle against the shepherds who destroy and scatter the flock is the closest prophetic parallel. Both prophets use the shepherd metaphor to indict Judah's rulers, and both promise God will raise up a righteous shepherd.

Jesus declares 'I am the good shepherd' against the backdrop of Ezekiel 34. His identification as the shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep claims to fulfill Ezekiel's promise of God himself tending the flock.

Peter instructs elders to 'be shepherds of God's flock,' echoing the shepherd language of Ezekiel 34 and applying it to church leadership. The 'Chief Shepherd' (archipoimenos) who will appear is Christ.

notes.note: The command to prophesy against the shepherds targets Israel's kings, priests, and leaders collectively. The shepherd metaphor was universally understood in the ancient world as referring to rulers.

The charges are specific: the shepherds eat the fat, wear the wool, and slaughter the choice animals, but they do not strengthen the weak, heal the sick, bind up the injured, bring back the strayed, or search for the lost. Leadership was exploitative rather than nurturing.
Without a shepherd, the flock was scattered 'over the whole face of the earth'—the exile itself. The sheep became food for wild beasts—the surrounding nations that preyed on helpless Israel.

God holds the shepherds accountable: because they fed themselves instead of the flock, they will answer to the Owner of the flock. The principle of delegated authority with accountability runs throughout Scripture.

God removes the shepherds from their position: 'I will remove them from tending the flock so that they can no longer feed themselves.' The Babylonian exile accomplished this by ending the Davidic monarchy and the pre-exilic priesthood.

Calvin applies this directly to pastors and church leaders: those who use their office for personal gain rather than the care of souls are the shepherds Ezekiel condemns. The warning applies to every generation of church leadership.
Calvin's list—strengthen, heal, bind up, bring back, search—provides a comprehensive job description for faithful leadership. Any leader who neglects these duties stands under Ezekiel's indictment.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'shepherds of Israel' (roʿey yisraʼel) encompasses the entire leadership class: kings, princes, priests, and prophets. The singular 'shepherd' metaphor applied to diverse leaders reflects the holistic view of governance in ancient Israel.

The four neglected duties (strengthen the weak, heal the sick, bind the injured, bring back the strayed) use specific agricultural vocabulary that would resonate with an audience familiar with actual shepherding practices.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies this as the 'anti-royal oracle' that formally indicts the failed monarchy. The shepherd metaphor allows Ezekiel to condemn the entire institution without naming individual kings. Block notes the irony: the shepherds have become predators—the very thing they were supposed to protect the flock from.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli highlights the catalog of neglected duties in v. 4 as a 'mirror for princes'—a genre known in ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature where the ideal ruler's responsibilities are enumerated. Ezekiel inverts the genre: instead of praising the ideal, he lists the reality of failure. Zimmerli argues this section provides the theological foundation for the subsequent promise of divine shepherding.

hist.context: Following the pivot of chapter 33, the restoration oracles begin with a devastating critique of Israel's failed leadership. The shepherd metaphor was ubiquitous in the ancient Near East: Hammurabi, Ashurbanipal, and Egyptian pharaohs all claimed the shepherd title. Jeremiah used the same metaphor against Judah's kings (Jer 23:1-4). Ezekiel's version is the most elaborate prophetic shepherd discourse in the Hebrew Bible and provides the immediate literary background for Jesus' Good Shepherd discourse in John 10.

sections.header: Verses 11-22 — The LORD as True Shepherd

heb.paragraph: YHWH declares 'I myself will search (darash) for my sheep and look after them' (v. 11). The verb darash carries the connotation of diligent, even desperate searching—not a casual glance but a thorough investigation. God does what the shepherds refused to do: he personally searches for every scattered sheep. This divine seeking is the theological foundation for Jesus' parable of the lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7), where the shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one. In Ezekiel, the scope is cosmic: God will gather sheep from all the countries where they were scattered on 'a day of clouds and darkness' (v. 12)—turning the Day-of-the-LORD imagery from judgment (ch. 30) into deliverance.

refs.note: David's psalm 'The LORD is my shepherd' is the poetic expression of the theology Ezekiel develops in narrative form. Both texts present God as the one who guides, provides, and protects.

Jesus' parable of the lost sheep directly echoes Ezekiel 34's language of God seeking and finding scattered sheep. The joy of the shepherd who finds the lost sheep reflects the divine emotion Ezekiel describes.

Jesus' separation of sheep and goats at the final judgment echoes Ezekiel 34:17-22, where God judges between sheep and sheep, between fat sheep who bully lean ones and the vulnerable who are pushed aside.

notes.note: God himself takes over the shepherding: 'I myself will search for my sheep.' The emphasis on 'I myself' (ani) three times in these verses stresses that God is not delegating—he is personally intervening.
God will bring them back to their own land and pasture them on the mountains of Israel. The 'good pasture' and 'rich grazing land' reverse the scattering described in vv. 5-6.

God will strengthen the weak, heal the sick, and bind up the injured—precisely the duties the human shepherds neglected. But he will also 'destroy the sleek and the strong'—judgment falls on those within the flock who exploited others.

God judges between sheep and sheep. The fat sheep muddied the water and trampled the pasture, leaving nothing for the lean sheep. This addresses social injustice within Israel: the wealthy and powerful exploited the vulnerable.

Calvin celebrates this passage as one of the most comforting in the prophets: when all human leaders fail, God steps in. The church should never despair of its condition because its true shepherd is not a man but the LORD himself.

Calvin notes the dual function of divine shepherding: tender care for the weak and stern judgment on the exploitative strong. God's love is not indiscriminate—it defends the vulnerable by confronting the oppressor.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'day of clouds and thick darkness' (yom ʿanan va-ʿaraphel) echoes the Day-of-the-LORD language from 30:3 and 34:12 inverts it: the day of darkness that brought judgment becomes the day when God rescues the scattered flock.

The distinction between 'sheep and sheep' (se le-se) and between 'rams and goats' distinguishes between the powerful and the weak within the community. This internal judgment addresses social justice.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block notes the comprehensive reversal: every failure cataloged in vv. 1-10 is systematically addressed by God's personal shepherding in vv. 11-16. He identifies vv. 17-22 as a secondary development: once God has gathered the flock, he must deal with internal predators—the fat sheep who exploited the lean. Block sees this as an early form of the 'remnant within the remnant' theology: even among the restored community, not all are righteous.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this as the theological high point of the chapter: the transformation of YHWH from judge of shepherds (vv. 1-10) to shepherd himself (vv. 11-16). The personal pronouns are emphatic throughout, stressing divine initiative. Zimmerli connects this to Ezekiel's broader theology of divine action: just as God himself will give a new heart (36:26) and breathe life into dry bones (37:5), so God himself becomes the shepherd because no human proxy is adequate.

hist.context: The pivot from indictment to promise: since human shepherds failed, YHWH himself will shepherd Israel. Every duty the shepherds neglected (v. 4) is now personally assumed by God (vv. 11-16). The reversal is systematic: where they scattered, God gathers; where they neglected the weak, God strengthens them; where they exploited the fat, God judges between fat and lean sheep (vv. 17-22). This section is unprecedented in prophetic literature: no other prophet develops the concept of God as active shepherd to this degree.

sections.header: Verses 23-31 — My Servant David: The Future Shepherd

heb.paragraph: The future Davidic ruler is called nasiʼ (prince) rather than melek (king). This deliberate demotion of title reflects Ezekiel's cautious attitude toward kingship after the institution's catastrophic failures. The nasiʼ is a shepherd under the Chief Shepherd (YHWH), not an autonomous sovereign. This distinction will be maintained in Ezekiel's temple vision (chs. 44-46), where the nasiʼ has specific but limited cultic responsibilities. The term signals a new model of leadership: delegated authority with accountability, not absolute power.

The berit shalom (covenant of peace) is YHWH's climactic promise: a new covenant that removes wild beasts, provides security, and causes the land to flourish. The word shalom encompasses far more than the absence of conflict—it denotes comprehensive well-being: physical safety, agricultural abundance, social harmony, and right relationship with God. This covenant of peace anticipates the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34 and finds its ultimate expression in the peace Christ brings (Eph 2:14).

refs.note: Jeremiah promises God will raise up for David a 'righteous Branch,' a king who will reign wisely. Both prophets anticipate an ideal Davidic ruler, and both deliberately use modest titles.
Jesus' declaration 'I am the good shepherd' claims to be the fulfillment of Ezekiel 34's promise. The 'one flock, one shepherd' of John 10:16 echoes Ezekiel's 'one shepherd' in 34:23.
Paul identifies Christ as 'our peace' who has made Jews and Gentiles 'one flock,' fulfilling the covenant of peace promised in Ezekiel 34:25.

notes.note: The 'one shepherd, my servant David' is the future messianic king—not literally David resurrected, but an ideal king from David's line. The title 'servant' (ebed) emphasizes humility and divine commission.
The title 'prince' (nasi) rather than 'king' (melek) is significant. After the failures of Judah's kings, Ezekiel envisions a more modest, accountable form of leadership.
The 'covenant of peace' promises comprehensive shalom: wild beasts removed, secure dwelling in the wilderness, no more fear. This covenant transforms the entire created order.
Showers of blessing, fruitful trees, abundant crops—the land itself participates in the restoration. The agricultural imagery reverses the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28.

The recognition formula concludes the chapter: Israel will know that the LORD is with them. The final image—'you are my sheep, the sheep of my pasture, and I am your God'—is the covenant formula in pastoral dress.

Calvin identifies 'my servant David' as Christ, the true shepherd who perfectly fulfills what no human king could. He argues that the monarchy's failure was providentially arranged to point Israel toward a greater shepherd.

Calvin sees the covenant of peace as encompassing not only spiritual blessings but also physical security and natural abundance. God's redemption addresses the whole of human existence, not merely the soul.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The reference to 'my servant David' (avdi david) is a messianic title. In prophetic usage, 'David' functions as a dynastic name referring to the ideal future king from the Davidic line (cf. Hos 3:5; Jer 30:9).
The 'covenant of peace' (berit shalom) envisions a comprehensive transformation of the created order. The removal of wild beasts echoes Leviticus 26:6, suggesting a reversal of the covenant curses.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block notes that the title nasiʼ (prince) rather than melek (king) represents a 'constitutional reform' of Israelite monarchy. The new Davidic ruler is explicitly subordinate to YHWH the Chief Shepherd, not an autonomous sovereign. Block connects the covenant of peace to the broader covenant theology of the book: this is not a return to the old covenant (which Israel broke) but a new arrangement where God ensures its success through his own direct action.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli reads the nasiʼ title as reflecting Ezekiel's 'theocratic correction' of the monarchy: YHWH is the true king (melek), and the Davidic figure is merely his deputy (nasiʼ). This represents a significant revision of pre-exilic royal theology, which had increasingly identified the king with divine prerogatives. Zimmerli sees this as one of the most important political-theological innovations of the exile.

hist.context: The restoration promise reaches its climax with three interconnected promises: (1) God will set over them one shepherd, 'my servant David' (v. 23), (2) God will make a 'covenant of peace' (v. 25), and (3) the land will become like the garden of Eden with showers of blessing (v. 26). The reference to 'David' is understood as a future Davidic king—an eschatological figure who will embody the ideal kingship that historical David only partially achieved. This is the most explicit messianic promise in Ezekiel.

themes.note: The first major restoration oracle: covenant, mercy, and justice are elevated as God promises to personally shepherd Israel and establish a new Davidic leader. Sovereignty undergirds everything—God acts because human leadership failed.

rows.label: vv.23-31
rows.text: My Servant David and the Covenant of Peace

lit.note: A three-movement restoration oracle: failed human shepherds are dismissed (vv. 1-10), YHWH assumes direct care (vv. 11-22), and a new Davidic shepherd is appointed under divine authority (vv. 23-31). The literary background for John 10.

hebtext.note: The roʿim (shepherds) is a standard ancient Near Eastern metaphor for rulers. In Mesopotamia, Sumerian and Akkadian kings bore the title 'shepherd of the people.' In Israel, God was the true shepherd (Ps 23:1) who delegated pastoral authority to human kings and leaders. Israel's leaders did none of these.

YHWH declares 'I myself will search (darash) for my sheep and look after them' (v. 11). The verb darash carries the connotation of diligent, even desperate searching—not a casual glance but a thorough investigation. God does what the shepherds refused to do: he personally searches for every scattered sheep. This divine seeking is the theological foundation for Jesus' parable of the lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7), where the shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one. In Ezekiel, the scope is cosmic: God will gather sheep from all the countries where they were scattered on 'a day of clouds and darkness' (v. 12)—turning the Day-of-the-LORD imagery from judgment (ch. 30) into deliverance.

The future Davidic ruler is called nasiʼ (prince) rather than melek (king). This deliberate demotion of title reflects Ezekiel's cautious attitude toward kingship after the institution's catastrophic failures. The nasiʼ is a shepherd under the Chief Shepherd (YHWH), not an autonomous sovereign. This distinction will be maintained in Ezekiel's temple vision (chs. 44-46), where the nasiʼ has specific but limited cultic responsibilities. The berit shalom (covenant of peace) is YHWH's climactic promise: a new covenant that removes wild beasts, provides security, and causes the land to flourish. The word shalom encompasses far more than the absence of conflict—it denotes comprehensive well-being: physical safety, agricultural abundance, social harmony, and right relationship with God. This covenant of peace anticipates the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34 and finds its ultimate expression in the peace Christ brings (Eph 2:14).

ppl.text: Job is a key figure in The Shepherds of Israel.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Shepherds of Israel

translations.text: You have not strengthened the weak or healed the sick or bound up the injured. You have not brought back the strays or searched for the lost. You have ruled them harshly and brutally.

The weak you have not strengthened, the sick you have not healed, the injured you have not bound up, the strayed you have not brought back, the lost you have not sought, and with force and harshness you have ruled them.

As surely as I live, declares the Sovereign Lord, because my flock lacks a shepherd and so has been plundered and has become food for all the wild animals, and because my shepherds did not search for my flock but cared for themselves rather than for my flock,

As I live, declares the Lord GOD, surely because my sheep have become a prey, and my sheep have become food for all the wild beasts, since there was no shepherd, and because my shepherds have not searched for my sheep, but the shepherds have fed themselves, and have not fed my sheep,

rec.quote: "The command to prophesy against the shepherds targets Israel's kings, priests, and leaders collectively. The shepherd metaphor was universally understood in the ancient world as referring to rulers."

thread.text: Jeremiah's oracle against the shepherds who destroy and scatter the flock is the closest prophetic parallel. Both prophets use the shepherd metaphor to indict Judah's rulers, and both promise God will raise up a righteous shepherd.

Jesus declares 'I am the good shepherd' against the backdrop of Ezekiel 34. His identification as the shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep claims to fulfill Ezekiel's promise of God himself tending the flock.

Peter instructs elders to 'be shepherds of God's flock,' echoing the shepherd language of Ezekiel 34 and applying it to church leadership. The 'Chief Shepherd' (archipoimenos) who will appear is Christ.
David's psalm 'The LORD is my shepherd' is the poetic expression of the theology Ezekiel develops in narrative form. Both texts present God as the one who guides, provides, and protects.

Jesus' parable of the lost sheep directly echoes Ezekiel 34's language of God seeking and finding scattered sheep. The joy of the shepherd who finds the lost sheep reflects the divine emotion Ezekiel describes.

Jesus' separation of sheep and goats at the final judgment echoes Ezekiel 34:17-22, where God judges between sheep and sheep, between fat sheep who bully lean ones and the vulnerable who are pushed aside.

coaching.tip: 'Woe to you shepherds of Israel who only take care of yourselves! Should not shepherds take care of the flock?' (v. 2). The indictment of failed leadership uses the metaphor Jesus adopts in John 10 ('I am the good shepherd'). Every charge — scattering the sheep, failing to heal, ruling harshly — is reversed in Jesus's self-description. Ezekiel 34 writes the job description; John 10 fills the position.

'I will place over them one shepherd, my servant David' (v. 23). David has been dead for 400 years; this isn't about the historical king but about his heir. The fusion of shepherd imagery, Davidic promise, and covenant restoration compresses the entire messianic expectation into one oracle. Ezekiel's 'David' carries the weight of every failed king and the hope of the one who won't fail.
